incorporations.original: he had induced his principal to employ me to polish slabs
incorporations.replacement: he had induced his employer to take me on for polishing slabs
incorporations.reason: 'Employer' is more natural than 'principal' for a sawmill owner; 'take me on for polishing' better captures the casual, piecework nature of the arrangement.

incorporations.original: He insisted on my isolation, on the ingratitude
incorporations.replacement: He dwelt upon my isolation, upon the ingratitude
incorporations.reason: 'Dwelt upon' renders 'insistette' with the connotation of lingering emphasis; the formal preposition 'upon' suits the elevated register.

incorporations.original: the indigence revealed by my home and my appearance
incorporations.replacement: the indigence revealed by my lodgings and my appearance
incorporations.reason: 'Lodgings' better conveys the temporary, rented quality of an exile's dwelling than the warmer 'home'; it matches the Italian 'abitazione' in its neutral formality.
'Station' captures the social rank implied by 'ambiente agiato' more precisely than the spatially-oriented 'environment'.

incorporations.original: a subtle and powerful coefficient: the honest conscience that changes the nature of sufferings
incorporations.replacement: one subtle and imperious coefficient: the honest conscience that transforms the sufferings
incorporations.reason: 'Imperious' renders 'prepotente' (overbearing, commanding) more precisely than 'powerful'. Claude's parallel 'transforms...converts' structure is more rhythmically effective than Gemini's 'changes the nature of...transforms'.

incorporations.original: at the price of my honesty
incorporations.replacement: at the price of my honour
incorporations.reason: The Italian 'onesta' can mean either honesty or honour; in context the Inspector is asking di Rudio to betray his comrades, making 'honour' the better sense.

incorporations.original: see his reflection in the serene eye
incorporations.replacement: see himself mirrored in the serene eye
incorporations.reason: 'See himself mirrored' preserves the reflexive force of 'specchiarsi' better than 'see his reflection', which externalises the image.

incorporations.original: a master at dissembling his state of mind with a smile
incorporations.replacement: a master at dissembling the state of his soul beneath a smile
incorporations.reason: The Italian 'animo' means soul or spirit, not merely mind. 'Beneath a smile' better conveys concealment than the neutral 'with a smile'.

incorporations.original: with a liberty not of the best taste, he could have compared me
incorporations.replacement: with a comparison of no very distinguished kind, he might have seen fit to liken me
incorporations.reason: The Italian 'con licenza non soverchiamente di buon genere' has an ironic, elaborate circumlocution that Claude's rendering preserves better than Gemini's flatter paraphrase.

incorporations.original: Charity — he said — let us not quibble over mere words
incorporations.replacement: Come now, — he said — let us not quarrel over mere words
incorporations.reason: Gemini mistranslated the Italian 'Carita' as the noun 'Charity'. Here it is an interjection meaning 'For pity's sake' or 'Come now' — an appeal for patience, not the theological virtue.

incorporations.original: seemed to want to stretch so as to touch his ears with their corners
incorporations.replacement: seemed on the point of stretching, at their very corners, as far as his ears
incorporations.reason: Claude's phrasing captures the grotesque, frozen quality of the Italian 'parve volessero spingersi' — the lips seemed about to stretch but did not; Gemini's 'wanted to stretch' over-personalises the lips.

incorporations.original: a long series of deceptions and violations, a Catholic coalition of superstitions and greed that would take morality for its banner or, rather, in the guise of a picklock
incorporations.replacement: a long succession of intrigues and outrages, a Catholic coalition of superstitions and cupidity which would take morality for its signboard or, more precisely, for a kind of skeleton-key
incorporations.reason: 'Raggiri' means intrigues rather than mere deceptions. 'Cupidigia' is best rendered by the Latinate 'cupidity'. 'Insegna' is literally a signboard, not a banner. 'Grimaldello' is a skeleton-key — Claude's phrasing is also syntactically clearer.

incorporations.original: two gentlemen might come to an understanding, despite the disparity of their ideas
incorporations.replacement: two men of honour might reach an understanding, despite the disparity of their views
incorporations.reason: 'Galantuomini' means men of honour, not merely gentlemen. The priest is making a moral appeal, not a social one.

incorporations.original: as a man of heart who could, by age, be your father
incorporations.replacement: as a man of feeling who might, by his age, be your father
incorporations.reason: 'Uomo di cuore' is idiomatically 'a man of feeling' in period English — 'man of heart' is a calque.

incorporations.original: It was the halter of need thrown around my neck!
incorporations.replacement: It was the noose of need cast about my neck!
incorporations.reason: While 'capestro' can mean halter, 'noose' better conveys the metaphorical strangling. 'Cast about' is more literary than 'thrown around'.
'Mi alzai di scatto' implies explosive, angry motion — 'sprang to my feet' is more physical than 'rose with a start', which implies surprise.
'Virile' in modern English carries sexual overtones absent from the Italian 'virili' (courageous, resolute); 'manly' is the better period equivalent. 'Eredita' is literally inheritance.

incorporations.replacement: It was the eve of Christmas
incorporations.reason: The Italian 'la vigilia del Natale' foregrounds 'vigilia' (eve, vigil) — 'the eve of Christmas' preserves this slightly formal, liturgical phrasing, fitting the bitter irony of the passage.

incorporations.original: I observed with dry eyes his little face, as white as the pillow on which it lay
incorporations.replacement: I gazed with dry eyes upon his little face, white as the pillow on which it rested
incorporations.reason: 'Gazed' carries the sustained, grief-stricken quality of the imperfect 'osservavo' better than the cooler 'observed'. 'Rested' is gentler than 'lay' for a dead child.

edgren_influences.note: Edgren hyphenates 'saw-mill'. The final translation follows this period-appropriate hyphenated form rather than the modern closed compound 'sawmill' used in the Gemini draft.

edgren_influences.edgren_definition: in essere (or mettersi) in —, be on the point of
edgren_influences.note: Edgren gives the exact idiom 'be on the point of' for 'in procinto'. Both drafts already used this phrasing, so the dictionary confirmed rather than redirected the choice.
Edgren confirms 'polish' as the primary sense of 'levigare'. Both drafts used 'polish'; the dictionary ruled out alternatives like 'smoothing' or 'finishing'.
